metadata.title: Paternalism in China Daily ’s coverage of Chinese Muslims (2001–2015)

abstract: This article explores the media representation of Muslims using critical discourse analysis (CDA). It emphasises the discursive construction of governmental paternalism that forms the dominant ideological disposition of China Daily's (CD) coverage. The results reveal how Chinese official English newspapers facilitate the government's dissemination of paternalistic discourse in the news of a large population of Chinese Muslims over the period. The investigation combines topic modelling with topos analysis to identify topics and topoi and to exhibit the ideology through the corpus compiled with CD's news about Chinese Muslims. Our findings both uncover the extent to which CD is used to promote paternalistic discourse by topic and reveal how paternalism is constructed by topoi. CD can be seen to strongly legitimise the paternalistic framework corresponding with the government's promotion of social development and security in the region. Reciprocally, most Chinese Muslims are portrayed as obedient and dependent Chinese citizens who benefit from the government's intervention.

sections.paper_id: Ye and Thomas - 2020 - Paternalism in China Daily ’s coverage of Chinese Muslims (2001–2015)
paragraphs: Chinese Muslims 1 are politically important minority groups within the population of China and have been subject to considerable recent media attention and salience. They are one of the largest ethnic communities in China, with a population that varies around 20 million (National Bureau of Statistics of China, 2019). 2 The community is comprised of ten ethnic minorities, in which the Hui and Uyghur account for 90%. In the mainstream Chinese media, Chinese Muslims have gained increasing attention and have been subject to increased salience due to recent unrest and governmental policies. Search in Factiva 3 demonstrates that the coverage has increased since the Urumqi Unrest, which occurred on 5 July 2009, with heavy casualties. The representation of Chinese Muslims in the media has attracted attention from researchers in journalism and communication fields but limited subsequent linguistic analysis has been undertaken. Both Luqiu and Yang (2018) and Zheng (2011) identify an overall negative framing of Chinese Muslims in the Chinese media. Terrorist framing of Uyghur rioters, conflicts, violence and economic underdevelopment in Xinjiang are commonly highlighted in Chinese state media discourse (e.g. People's Daily).

Chinese Muslims are governed within a paternalist framework by the Chinese government from a socio-political perspective. Investment from the Chinese government in the regional economy of Xinjiang demonstrates the implementation of governmental paternalism, such as through the 'Great Western Development' and 'Belt and Road Initiative'. After the Urumqi Unrest on 5 July 2009, the government underlined both security and economic development in Xinjiang in relation to Chinese Muslims (Anand, 2018;Holdstock, 2015). Shan and Weng (2010) and Mackerras (2014) claim that economic development has been accelerated following ethnic tensions. Religious policies for Muslims also indicate paternalism, given that Islamism in China 'is destined for longterm coexistence with the Party' (Kung, 2006: 385).

The research above indicates the need for further enquiry into official governmental media channels to understand the discursive construction of political paternalism of national policies. Chinese official media channels and state discourse are believed to share ideological congruency (Liu, 2012;Stone, 1994). Therefore, the article seeks to investigate how governmental paternalism is represented in CD's coverage of Chinese Muslims through two research questions. First, to what extent does CD utilise a paternalistic discourse regarding Chinese Muslims. Second, how is this paternalistic discursive approach constructed within the CD's coverage? Topics and argumentation are salient discursive strategies to express ideologies in critical discourse analysis (Van Dijk, 2009;Wodak, 2001). So, by investigating the answers to the questions, the research hopes to unravel how governmental paternalism is represented in the data.

sections.paper_id: Ye and Thomas - 2020 - Paternalism in China Daily ’s coverage of Chinese Muslims (2001–2015)
paragraphs: Paternalism is prevalent in Chinese government policy (Anand, 2018;Cheng et al., 2017;Wang and Liu, 2018), which has been studied extensively in political science but seemingly under-investigated regarding how it is construed in discourse. As an ideology, it is defined as the restriction of an individual's autonomy in favour of governmental policies to promote citizens' welfare (Le Grand and New, 2015). Le Grand and New's definition has two basic elements of seeking to do good to recipients and perceiving the incompetence of individuals. This is also largely in line with previous studies of the ideology (e.g. Dworkin, 1972). Le Grand and New's definition is used for the current research.

Governmental paternalism, in policy terms, has several distinct and idiosyncratic features in the Chinese context in line with Le Grand and New's definition. First, officials in China have been named as parent officials, who take responsibility for citizens (Cheng et al., 2017). The second feature is 'putting people first' which primarily seeks solutions for Chinese citizens (Wang and Liu, 2018). Saich (2012) adds that such a principle requires the government to get close to people and prioritises people's 'needs' and 'benefits'. Officials are encouraged to 'be more responsive and take care of those in need' (Saich, 2012: 11) and especially to offer benefits to ordinary people and adopt the mentality of taking care of ordinary people (p. 12). He adds that economic status in China and the improvement of people's livelihoods are the essential realisations of 'people first' rule. From the characteristics outlined above, the acts of the Chinese government that are assumed to be beneficial to Chinese citizens (e.g. economic development) restrict people's autonomy without their consent. Third, support from citizens to the Chinese government and its policies are significant in Chinese governmental paternalism. This feature is related to filial piety in China that emphasises respect, obedience and support from citizens to the government and its paternalistic acts. It also requires 'the hierarchy of values and relationship as well as cultural assimilation to achieve harmony' (Wang and Liu, 2018: 24). Therefore, it is common in China to propagandise people's support for Beijing as a dimension of paternalism.

In the current study, CD is the data source for the investigation of governmental paternalism in the official media discourse towards Chinese Muslims. CD is an official English-language newspaper in China, which plays a particular role in portraying China and articulating the Chinese government's voice to the international community. Researchers in journalism and communication believe that this broadsheet has become a key source to express national ideologies to global readers (Liu, 2012;Stone, 1994). It is also indicated that CD is a resource that represents the ideology of the Chinese government (e.g. governmental paternalism) and its policies (Lee, 2010;Song et al., 2019). So, it can be considered as an official newspaper with a strong alignment with Chinese state discourse. CD has a global reach, and so coverage can be considered more salient at a global level. In a discussion about the readership of Chinese national broadsheets overseas, CD is significant for the US government to acquire the latest outlook from China, though it is not considered as central attraction to most western consumers (Liu, 2012).

paragraphs: This study was conducted with corpus-assisted discourse analysis (CADS), which has gained attention recently in the field of discourse analysis. It is believed that corpus analysis shares the characteristics commonly ascribed to approaches in science and specifically objectivity, through topic modelling analysis, concordance analysis and so on (Baker, 2006;Partington et al., 2013). With the foci of repetitive patterns in big textual data, corpus analysis can compensate for the commonly acknowledged potential flaws in taking exclusively qualitative approaches to discourse analysis, for example, subjectivity and over-interpretation (Blommaert, 2005), and indicates ideologies (e.g. governmental paternalism) inherent in media discourse (Baker, 2010).

CADS has been applied extensively in the investigation of ideologies in media discourse, combined with qualitative analysis (e.g. Baker, 2010). The method encompasses technologically sophisticated methods for analysis, such as semantic domain analysis to investigate the representation of Muslim women in media (Al-Hejin, 2015) and topic modelling analysis for the portrayal of Muslims in social media discourse (Törnberg and Törnberg, 2016a). These studies demonstrate that a discourse analysis study informed by corpus analysis is a productive approach. In order to discern the extent of the ideology of governmental paternalism in media discourse, we applied a post-disciplinary perspective to techniques -corpus tools were used flexibly and recursively when investigation needs.

sections.paper_id: Ye and Thomas - 2020 - Paternalism in China Daily ’s coverage of Chinese Muslims (2001–2015)
paragraphs: The data for this study come from a corpus of 747 news articles about Chinese Muslims in CD, in which all the news was published from 11 September 2001 to the end of 2015. The 9/11 attack was taken as a starting point since the coverage about Muslims increased dramatically after the attack (e.g. Baker et al., 2013). The end of 2015 was the cut point of data collection since conflicts in Xinjiang dramatically decreased after 2016 (BBC, 2016). From this socio-political period between 2001 and 2015, our project reveals how the government and over 20 million Chinese Muslims are portrayed. The texts were first collated to compile two news archives by keyword searching of 'Chinese Muslim(s)', 'Uighur(s)', 'Uyghur(s)', 'Ethnic Hui' and so forth. 4 All the word items are usually used to describe Chinese Muslims. After the extraction, the news pieces irrelevant to the research were manually deleted.

A CD corpus of 485,327 words was compiled. In order to have a comprehensive picture of how paternalism is represented in the news discourse, news texts of all genres were included in the data. More than 95% of the chosen texts were news reports, which can appear in the columns of Business, Culture and Government in CD. News coverage of other genres (e.g. editorial, opinions) account for 5% of the data, and it is made explicit by the newspaper per se. About 67.17% out of 747 pieces of news in the CD originally come from CD. CD also represents views from other official newspapers in China, such as Xinhua and Beijing Weekly.

sections.paper_id: Ye and Thomas - 2020 - Paternalism in China Daily ’s coverage of Chinese Muslims (2001–2015)
paragraphs: The data analysis was divided into three stages. The data were initially investigated manually before the use of corpus analysis. Corpus analysis was conducted to signify topics pertaining to governmental paternalism, and this enquiry addresses the question of what topics in the CD elicit governmental paternalism. Results from corpus analysis were further examined by qualitative analysis, construing its linguistic realisation in Chinese media discourse about Chinese Muslims.

Initially, 30 news texts in the CD were randomly selected and read by the analysts, which ensures an overview of pronounced topics in the data suggestive of governmental paternalism in the media discourse. For instance, in a news article titled Chinese Muslims head for Mecca, 13,800 Chinese Muslims were helped by the Chinese government to participate in Mecca pilgrimage in 2012. The subsidence from the Chinese government to the religious life of its fellow Muslim citizens exhibits paternalistic characteristics.

Topic modelling is the primary corpus analytical method in the analysis, which aims at identifying the topics in the CD corpus exhibiting governmental paternalism (i.e. paternalistic discourse), such as subsidence from the government towards Muslims' pilgrimage (e.g. 'state-organised visits to Mecca for the annual hajj pilgrimage'). Ideologies are inherent in thematic structures in discourse, that is, topics in discourse (Van Dijk, 2009). Topic modelling can discover topics in discourse in an inductive manner, which allows for the retrieval of hidden topics in corpus otherwise overlooked (Törnberg and Törnberg, 2016b) and express them with topic indicators (see Appendix 1). This study used LDA (i.e. Latent Dirichlet Allocation) to run topic modelling, which is a commonly used algorithm within this method. We used GUI for Mallet's implementation of LDA, an online topic modelling tool. 5 Two parameters were important in the implementation. First, in line with Jockers ( 2013) and the length of most articles in the CD, each document in the analysis lies within the word limit of 500-1000 words. Extremely long and short texts were, respectively, cut and combined. For instance, a 1100-word text was divided into two documents of 1000 words and 100 words. The 100 words were then combined into another document with significantly less than 500 words. Second, in terms of LDA, the number of topics needed to be set before any analysis was applied using either statistics or researchers' judgement. We followed Törnberg and Törnberg (2016a) who suggest experimenting with different topic numbers to find the most interpretable results. It was found that setting the number at 22 would be optimal for generating the most meaningful interpretation as to what topics were marked in the corpus. Setting a different number of topics, we could find multiple meanings in one topic. The automatically generated topics were unnamed and thus were labelled by the researchers. We examined the top 20 high-frequency indicators in each topic to decide whether the topic could reliably be indicative of governmental paternalism. The examination of the number of indicators lightly varying at either side of the figure of 20 did not significantly impact the results.

Having identified topics and their significance in the CD relevant to governmental paternalism, we then analysed the ideological construction by argumentative strategies. Ten discourse sections that include topic indicators were selected randomly for each topic related to the ideology, and 90 discourse sections were examined qualitatively. Such investigations were conducted to examine topoi in paternalistic discourse. They would link the argument together to address the ideological qualities of the content in the CD.

Topoi are prevalent in media or political discourses for persuasion, as have been evidenced in Wodak (2011) and Wright and Brookes (2019). The use of topoi can present positive self-presentation, negative-other presentation, revealing differences/similarities, warning of dangers and discrediting opponents (Wodak et al., 2009). For the identification of topoi, we followed the taxonomy developed in Wodak (2001Wodak ( , 2009) that provides a helpful interpretive framework, for example, the topos of advantage and the topos of responsibility. Notably, we found another topos -the topos of happiness recurrent in our data but no appearance in Wodak's taxonomy. The topos, as is explored in Allen (2014), expresses a positive mood (e.g. satisfaction and pleasure) to accomplish persuasion. This scheme is overlapping with the topos of advantage since both argumentative strategies stress positivity to ingroups for a convincing conclusion (e.g. the implementation of an initiative). However, the topos of happiness focuses on positive emotions of people, while benefits to individuals (e.g. the betterment of social security) are stressed with the topos of advantage. Therefore, we explored the argumentative schemes that formulate governmental paternalism in the corpus.

Subjectivity in the analysis was reduced but not excluded. Researchers have independently read through the news articles to identify paternalistic discourse in the CD, specified the number of topics and topic names and identified the types of topoi in paternalistic discourse. Homogeneous results were re-examined until a consensus was achieved. Topoi are presented in the discourse in flexible linguistic patterns, so the naming and identification process cannot avoid subjectivity. To address this issue, typical topoi we observed were elaborated with examples.
sections.text:
The data analysis was divided into three stages. The data were initially investigated manually before the use of corpus analysis. Corpus analysis was conducted to signify topics pertaining to governmental paternalism, and this enquiry addresses the question of what topics in the CD elicit governmental paternalism. Results from corpus analysis were further examined by qualitative analysis, construing its linguistic realisation in Chinese media discourse about Chinese Muslims.

Initially, 30 news texts in the CD were randomly selected and read by the analysts, which ensures an overview of pronounced topics in the data suggestive of governmental paternalism in the media discourse. For instance, in a news article titled Chinese Muslims head for Mecca, 13,800 Chinese Muslims were helped by the Chinese government to participate in Mecca pilgrimage in 2012. The subsidence from the Chinese government to the religious life of its fellow Muslim citizens exhibits paternalistic characteristics.

Topic modelling is the primary corpus analytical method in the analysis, which aims at identifying the topics in the CD corpus exhibiting governmental paternalism (i.e. paternalistic discourse), such as subsidence from the government towards Muslims' pilgrimage (e.g. 'state-organised visits to Mecca for the annual hajj pilgrimage'). Ideologies are inherent in thematic structures in discourse, that is, topics in discourse (Van Dijk, 2009). Topic modelling can discover topics in discourse in an inductive manner, which allows for the retrieval of hidden topics in corpus otherwise overlooked (Törnberg and Törnberg, 2016b) and express them with topic indicators (see Appendix 1). This study used LDA (i.e. Latent Dirichlet Allocation) to run topic modelling, which is a commonly used algorithm within this method. We used GUI for Mallet's implementation of LDA, an online topic modelling tool. 5 Two parameters were important in the implementation. First, in line with Jockers ( 2013) and the length of most articles in the CD, each document in the analysis lies within the word limit of 500-1000 words. Extremely long and short texts were, respectively, cut and combined. For instance, a 1100-word text was divided into two documents of 1000 words and 100 words. The 100 words were then combined into another document with significantly less than 500 words. Second, in terms of LDA, the number of topics needed to be set before any analysis was applied using either statistics or researchers' judgement. We followed Törnberg and Törnberg (2016a) who suggest experimenting with different topic numbers to find the most interpretable results. It was found that setting the number at 22 would be optimal for generating the most meaningful interpretation as to what topics were marked in the corpus. Setting a different number of topics, we could find multiple meanings in one topic. The automatically generated topics were unnamed and thus were labelled by the researchers. We examined the top 20 high-frequency indicators in each topic to decide whether the topic could reliably be indicative of governmental paternalism. sections.paper_id: Ye and Thomas - 2020 - Paternalism in China Daily ’s coverage of Chinese Muslims (2001–2015)
paragraphs: Governmental paternalism towards Chinese Muslims is elicited in 10 topics that are grouped as four topic categories; a topic category comprises topics with similar meaning (Figure 1). Statistically, the contribution value of these topic categories is 0.45 out of 1, which indicates the importance of these topics in the CD corpus at the discourse level and a high degree of discursive paternalism in CD's coverage of Chinese Muslims. In addition to these topic categories, there are 5 topic categories and 13 topics in the CD, such as community affairs that illustrate life stories of Chinese Muslims.

Chinese governmental paternalism is primarily revealed through the topic category of social transformation that emphasises Beijing's subsidisation to improve Chinese Muslim society. The newspaper highlights the improvement in living standard (e.g. 'home', 'work'), economy ('economy', 'investment'), education ('school', 'teach') and healthcare system ('public', 'health') with the overall contribution value of these topics in corpus being 0.21 (⩾0.05 6 ). Contribution value of a topic indicates its importance in the corpus at the discourse level, and thus 0.21 elucidates CD's focus on social transformation in the coverage of Chinese Muslims, and on Chinese Muslims as recipients of the government's help to improve their lives. An article of 2013 cites a top Chinese official: 'Xinjiang should be established as a western port for China's foreign trade' (24 September). Economic support and achievements have the highest contribution value of 0.1 among the four topics in the CD, and such a focus in corpus agrees with Chinese national policies concerning Chinese Muslims -the Chinese government pays attention to the economic development in Xinjiang with the aim of promoting the modernisation of the region (Anand, 2018;Mackerras, 2014). The list of topic indicators is provided in Appendix 1.

The second topic category is Islam as a religion in the corpus. Beijing helps ethnic Muslims with religious freedom, such as the organisation of chartered flights or adjusting the market price of lamb during Ramadan. The topics of Muslim rituals and figures (e.g. 'pilgrimage' and 'halal') are included in the category, with the total contribution value of 0.12 (⩾0.05) (Figure 1). There are many examples in the text, for instance, a journalist wrote on 17 September 2014 that 'the total number of Chinese pilgrims who go to Mecca will exceed 14,000 on a government-organised trip this year' (also see Example 2). The topic salience in the corpus reflects Beijing's respect for Islam and assistance to Chinese Muslims for the practice of Islam, thus reflecting governmental paternalism in such coverage. Also, as suggested from the topic category, CD helps Beijing to promote the idea that religious freedom should be implemented through government assistance (Kung, 2006). These topic categories reveal that, to a large extent, Chinese Muslims are projected as ingroup members to the Chinese government -Chinese citizens needing and enjoying governmental assistance. This is important because such identification is typical of the discursive traits pertaining to paternalistic ideology (cf. Le Grand and New, 2015).

The topic categories of terrorism/violence and reaction to terrorism/violence are inherent to Chinese governmental paternalism, whereby journalists set the boundary between most Chinese Muslims as an ingroup versus a few Chinese Muslims as an outgroup to the government. The ideology is reflected by addressing the vulnerability of citizens (Le Grand and New, 2015). More specifically, securitisation has been highlighted by the Chinese government in its paternalistic policies in Xinjiang, which emphasised the relative powerlessness of Chinese Muslim citizens, underlined the security challenges of Xinjiang, as well as justifying Beijing's subsequent policies towards Chinese Muslims (Anand, 2018). As was revealed in topic modelling analysis, the topic category of violence/violence has the contribution value of 0.07 (⩾0.05) in the corpus. Correspondents cover terrorist acts inclusive of terms such as 'terrorist', 'killed' and 'attack', as well as reporting on consequences of these acts to the majority of Chinese Muslim citizens (e.g. 'security' and 'injuries'). For instance, a scholar on Xinjiang issues denounces the slaughter of Chinese citizens in a news piece (China Daily, 2013). The foci on violent acts and their effects on most Chinese Muslims are portrayed as justifying Beijing's protective initiative and reveal Beijing's concerns about Chinese Muslim citizens, strongly indicating paternalistic approaches and the inclusion of the group within the national community. However, the reference to 'terrorist' indicates that parts of the community are politicised as an outgroup and are not considered a part of Chinese Muslims. Moreover, the category of reaction to attacks/violence (e.g. 'police', 'crack' and 'down') with a contribution value of 0.05 also indicates a high degree of paternalism in the news. As was shown from an official's voice from Beijing 'measures should be taken to prevent more terror attacks and guarantee people's safety' (also see Example 4), the journalist distinguishes between 'people' (i.e. Chinese Muslims) as an ingroup to the government and 'terror' -the juxtaposition of terrorism with religious identity -as an outgroup. There is both a legitimisation of Chinese Muslims as a conceived collective whole and isolation of its detracting elements -some Chinese Muslims participating in violence. The identification of an ingroup to Beijing and the distinction made for the ideological detractors indicate paternalism and provide, through discursive constructions, the need and obligation for the government to protect 'good' citizens.

sections.paper_id: Ye and Thomas - 2020 - Paternalism in China Daily ’s coverage of Chinese Muslims (2001–2015)
sections.header_raw: Paternalism and topoi in the CD
sections.header_norm: paternalism and topoi in the cd
paragraphs: Topoi in the CD were investigated for the linguistic construction of governmental paternalism in the media discourse towards Chinese Muslims. We probed into how argumentative strategies are used to construct and portray paternalism towards these citizens in the four topic categories previously highlighted. We also graded the relevance of these topoi to the formulation of 'paternalism' in the discourse. The contribution value of a topic category that entails the frequent occurrence of a specific topos indicates to what extent the topos helps with the expression of paternalism in the corpus (Figure 2).

The topos of advantage is an essential strategy in the CD corpus that portrays the majority of Chinese Muslims as recipients of Beijing's help. The topos is typified by the following condition: if an advantageous outcome is generated, actions will be resumed. So, writers in discourse typically underpin the benefits to a social community to imply the necessity for the implementation of a practice (Wodak, 2001(Wodak, , 2009)). In the CD, the topos of advantage is identified in most of the topics previously discussed, and the contribution value of these topics is 0.38 out of 1.04. Thus, the topos is used significantly to construct paternalism in the corpus (see Figure 2). With the topos, journalists highlight the government's supportive intervention in the development of the Chinese Muslim community as well as the benefits given to its citizens (cf. Le Grand and New, 2015). Also, it reflects the prioritisation of needs towards the minorities that reflects the government's 'putting people first' principle (Wang and Liu, 2018). The topos thus clearly contributes to building the Chinese government's father-like status and paternalistic framing of responsibilities towards these citizens. The providing of benefits to the Chinese Muslims also indicates a need to intervene in the private lives of the ingroup and thus assumes the mantel of social guardianship. Therefore, with both the security aspect and the provision of care, we can say that paternalism is ideologically embedded in the CD's representation. To illustrate, Example 1 shows Beijing's supportive policies towards Chinese Muslims in the economic sphere.

Example 1 URUMQI -China's far western Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region is becoming better connected with the international community after the implementation of the Silk Road Economic 7 Belt. (11 July 2015) The topos of advantage is manifested by the narrative of Beijing's launching of the Silk Road initiative and its goodwill to Chinese Muslims. As suggested by the topic indicators 'economic' and 'the implementation of Silk Road Economic Belt', Beijing has undertaken economic supporting initiatives for the social transformation in Xinjiang with the largest Chinese Muslim population. Notably, the supporting action is nominalised as 'the implementation of Silk Road Economic Belt'. Fairclough (1989) claims that the use of nominalisation serves to leave the attribution of responsibility unclear. Through this expression, the government's initiative in the supportive economic policy is backgrounded, and its interference in the regional economy is downplayed. Beijing is thus portrayed as a considerate father-like figure, assisting the regional economy with little interference in the autonomy of its citizens. The supportive economic measures modernise the local economy and aid local Chinese Muslim populations to broaden their capacity for interaction with the rest of the world since Xinjiang becomes 'better connected with the international community'. Xinjiang has a large population of Muslim citizens, and it is known as a geographically remote region in Western China with insufficient communication with the outside world. So, it is imaginable that increasing socialisation meets the need for a large population of Muslim residents. Based on the analysis, the topos indicates the Chinese government's paternalistic care to Chinese Muslims with 'the Silk Road Economic Belt', or the Belt Road Initiative. We probed into how argumentative strategies are used to construct and portray paternalism towards these citizens in the four topic categories previously highlighted. We also graded the relevance of these topoi to the formulation of 'paternalism' in the discourse. The contribution value of a topic category that entails the frequent occurrence of a specific topos indicates to what extent the topos helps with the expression of paternalism in the corpus (Figure 2).

The topos of advantage is an essential strategy in the CD corpus that portrays the majority of Chinese Muslims as recipients of Beijing's help. The topos is typified by the following condition: if an advantageous outcome is generated, actions will be resumed. In the CD, the topos of advantage is identified in most of the topics previously discussed, and the contribution value of these topics is 0.38 out of 1.04. Thus, the topos is used significantly to construct paternalism in the corpus (see Figure 2). With the topos, journalists highlight the government's supportive intervention in the development of the Chinese Muslim community as well as the benefits given to its citizens (cf. Le Grand and New, 2015). Also, it reflects the prioritisation of needs towards the minorities that reflects the government's 'putting people first' principle (Wang and Liu, 2018). The topos thus clearly contributes to building the Chinese government's father-like status and paternalistic framing of responsibilities towards these citizens. The providing of benefits to the Chinese Muslims also indicates a need to intervene in the private lives of the ingroup and thus assumes the mantel of social guardianship. Therefore, with both the security aspect and the provision of care, we can say that paternalism is ideologically embedded in the CD's representation. To illustrate, Example 1 shows Beijing's supportive policies towards Chinese Muslims in the economic sphere.

sections.paper_id: Ye and Thomas - 2020 - Paternalism in China Daily ’s coverage of Chinese Muslims (2001–2015)
paragraphs: The family of six had been living in a rented apartment that had barely enough space, Xirip said. She is happy to see local governments are now helping the family with two low-rent apartments. (5 February 2014) Chinese Muslims all explicitly convey sentiments consistent with the topos of happiness in these examples -'she is happy'. The clear expression of happiness distinguishes most Muslim citizens from the small number who are engaged in violence (cf. Example 4).

In the above example, satisfaction is juxtaposed with the benefits of national policies towards Chinese Muslims. Contentment among Xirip and help from Beijing are obvious in Example 2. 'Two low-rent apartments' that are supported by the 'local government' and give satisfaction to the Muslim 'family of six'. In a reported speech, the writer claims that 'she (Xirip) is happy to see' the betterment of the living standard in her family (i.e. the topos of advantage) that is achieved by governmental subsidisation to family's living conditions and reveals her submissiveness to the government. The writer also makes it explicit that Beijing's engagement addresses the needs of her family since they 'had been living in a rented apartment that had barely enough space'. In this sense, filial piety from Xirip is related to the government's prioritisation of people's necessities. So, the appreciation of the ingroup justifies the government's approaches by showing that the view of guardianship is positive, and intervention is mutually supported. The combined use of the topos of happiness and advantage here exemplifies filial piety (e.g. respect and submissiveness) from the Muslim citizens towards the government. Filial piety pertains to Chinese national policy of 'putting people first' -the government supports Chinese Muslims through addressing their needs and solving their difficulties in daily life. The Chinese government is thus portrayed as paternal to a largely obedient Chinese Muslim citizenry.

The topos of responsibility is frequently identified in CD's coverage of Chinese Muslims to reveal the government's responsibilities and obligations to assist and protect its citizens and identify citizens as an ingroup to the government. The topos of responsibility views that because a group or person is responsible for the way things are, that group or person should act to put things right (Wodak, 2001). In the corpus, this argumentative strategy is identified in the topics of social transformation and Beijing's responses to terrorist attacks with the contribution value of 0.26 (out of 1.04) (see Figure 2). The paternalistic tradition from the Chinese government maintains the principle of parent officials (i.e. Fumu guan) -fulfilling the duty to take care of its citizens (Cheng et al., 2017;Ling, 1994). CD combines a dual approach of mutually inclusive support for transformative social policies and action on terror directed at the outgroup. This two-fold discursive strategy is the dominant approach of CD in legitimising paternalism.

Modal verbs are typical in the presentation of the topos of responsibility (see Wright and Brookes, 2019). The view is correspondent with Fairclough (2000) who believes that the use of modal verbs indicates 'authoritarian language' and suggests 'moral toughness' (p. 108). In paternalistic discourse here, we specifically investigated the modal verb 'should' in the conveyance of Beijing's responsibility in assisting its Muslim citizens because of the higher frequency of 'should' (frequency = 709) than other modal verbs in the CD (e.g. 'must', frequency = 354; 'need', frequency = 480). The modal verb has both deontic and epistemic meaning, which respectively refers to obligations that ground demand in the subjectivity of the speaker and possibilities that intermediate the certainty and increase the objectivity of information or knowledge. In the corpus, the deontic 'should' is mostly related to the topos of responsibility. It conveys Beijing's subjectivity and determination to get involved in Muslims' lives in a supportive way, reinforces an ingroup identity of most Chinese Muslims and provides the opportunity structure for integration and collusion. For instance, in Example 3, the Chinese government is described as embracing the duty of curbing Chinese Muslim terrorism; a small number of Chinese Muslims participating in the Kashgar violence and the group is to be derided and no longer included in the Chinese Muslim ingroup.

Example 3 'Strong and effective measures should be taken to prevent more terror attacks and guarantee people's safety, their assets and regional stability', he said. '(We should) resolutely punish terrorists according to the law, crackdown on terror activities, resolutely crack down on extreme religious forces and effectively contain illegal religious activities'. (2 August 2011) In the voice of former Vice-Premier Zhang Gaoli, 'crackdown' makes the extract an anti-terror narrative. In the extract of anti-terrorism, he demonises the Muslims who have participated in the violence: 'terrorists', 'extreme religious forces' and conduct 'illegal religious activities'. Specifically, the repeated labels of 'extreme' and 'religious' remind people of fundamentalism and global terrorism. The pejorative features portray the minority of Muslims as a peril to the majority of Chinese Muslims, that is, 'people', which subsidise the government in excluding the few outgroup members from majority ingroup members.

In the narrative of Beijing's anti-terror activities, the topos of responsibility is first of all realised by two 'shoulds' whereby curbing terrorism is seen as an obligation for the Chinese government after violent attacks, and the objectified need is indicative of intervention. Despite the first 'should' appearing in the passive voice, the responsibility of taking 'strong and effective measures' against terrorism is directed from the Chinese government through Zhang's voice. These measures are claimed to 'guarantee people's safety, their assets and regional stability', thus beneficial to Chinese Muslims citizens in Xinjiang. Once again, the benefits presented in the discourse provide justification for the government's interventionist policies. The next 'should' elaborates on the prevention of terrorism with strong measures, which reinforces Beijing's father-like status. With the inclusive 'we' and 'should', the official treats it as the responsibility to protect its ingroup members from terrorism.

A list of three is used to strengthen Beijing's duty to prevent terrorism after a series of attacks, as well as to level-up Muslims' identity as an ingroup to the government. By definition, speakers in political speeches usually use the parataxis of at least three actions with similar patterns to reinforce the rhetorical force of delivery and increase persuasive qualities (Atkinson, 1989). The journalist in such an example juxtaposes 'punish(ing) terrorists', 'crack(ing) down on terror activists', 'crack(ing) down on' extremists and 'contain(ing) illegal religious activities'. These four actions are listed to impress international readers and show that the Chinese government will bring lawless and evil terrorists to justice for safety and stability in Chinese Muslim areas.

The topos of reality is used in the CD to reveal governmental paternalism. According to Wodak (2011), the scheme is typically the documentation of terrible social situations in discourse (e.g. poverty) and serves to justify the need for specific actions or decisions. In the CD corpus, the topos is used frequently to frame the topic category of terrorism/violence with the contribution value of 0.07 (out of 1.04) (Figure 2), and topic indicators include 'attacks', 'killed' and 'terrorists'. Regarding the use of the topos of reality, journalists emphasise actions in violence and consequences, and the scenarios position most Chinese Muslims as being in a worrying and dangerous situation. The plight of Chinese Muslims in the broadsheet justifies the government's actions to punish terrorists, who constitute a minority within the Chinese Muslim population. Simply put, the topos of reality reveals the vulnerability of a large population of Chinese Muslim citizens bred by the few evil and lawless Chinese Muslims and the needing of governmental interventions.

The topos thus construes governmental anti-terror campaigns that Beijing prioritises the necessity of its ingroup members and bring them security as well as constructs paternalism (cf. Le Grand and New, 2015;Saich, 2012;Wang and Liu, 2018). Example 4 about violence and terrorism elaborates on the use of the topos of reality to portray the victimised Chinese Muslim majority and the paternalistic Chinese government; topic indicators include 'riot', 'terrorist', 'extremist' and 'destroyed'. 'The riot has destroyed the spiritual support with which the terrorist, separatist and extremist forces cheated the people to participate in the so-called "Jihad"', Wang said. (7 July 2009) In the example, harm caused by terrorists to the majority of Chinese Muslim citizens is marked in the voice of 'Xinjiang Communist Party of China (CPC) chief'. They are first expressed in related to 'riot' and 'Jihad' and explicitly labelled as 'terrorist, separatist and extremist', and the labels explicate their Muslim identity and malicious nature. These terrorists, separatist and extremist Chinese Muslims are harmful to the Muslim majority in China because they 'destroyed the spiritual support' and 'cheated the people to participate in the so-called "Jihad"'. In other words, these Muslims are pictured as betraying Islam and misleading the majority (i.e. 'the people') to fundamentalism. The official's voice and the statement of harm caused by this minority to the Chinese Muslim majority build a boundary between the majority as an ingroup and the minority outgroup. CD justifies the necessity for the government to protect the ingroup by revealing its members' mental victimisation to the evil group. So, the topos of reality here predicts the government's activities of anti-terrorism previously discussed, through which the ideology is discernible.

For instance, in Example 3, the Chinese government is described as embracing the duty of curbing Chinese Muslim terrorism; a small number of Chinese Muslims participating in the Kashgar violence and the group is to be derided and no longer included in the Chinese Muslim ingroup.

Example 3 'Strong and effective measures should be taken to prevent more terror attacks and guarantee people's safety, their assets and regional stability', he said. '(We should) resolutely punish terrorists according to the law, crackdown on terror activities, resolutely crack down on extreme religious forces and effectively contain illegal religious activities'. (2 August 2011) In the voice of former Vice-Premier Zhang Gaoli, 'crackdown' makes the extract an anti-terror narrative. In the extract of anti-terrorism, he demonises the Muslims who have participated in the violence: 'terrorists', 'extreme religious forces' and conduct 'illegal religious activities'. Specifically, the repeated labels of 'extreme' and 'religious' remind people of fundamentalism and global terrorism. The pejorative features portray the minority of Muslims as a peril to the majority of Chinese Muslims, that is, 'people', which subsidise the government in excluding the few outgroup members from majority ingroup members.

In the narrative of Beijing's anti-terror activities, the topos of responsibility is first of all realised by two 'shoulds' whereby curbing terrorism is seen as an obligation for the Chinese government after violent attacks, and the objectified need is indicative of intervention. Despite the first 'should' appearing in the passive voice, the responsibility of taking 'strong and effective measures' against terrorism is directed from the Chinese government through Zhang's voice. These measures are claimed to 'guarantee people's safety, their assets and regional stability', thus beneficial to Chinese Muslims citizens in Xinjiang. Once again, the benefits presented in the discourse provide justification for the government's interventionist policies. The next 'should' elaborates on the prevention of terrorism with strong measures, which reinforces Beijing's father-like status. With the inclusive 'we' and 'should', the official treats it as the responsibility to protect its ingroup members from terrorism.

A list of three is used to strengthen Beijing's duty to prevent terrorism after a series of attacks, as well as to level-up Muslims' identity as an ingroup to the government. By definition, speakers in political speeches usually use the parataxis of at least three actions with similar patterns to reinforce the rhetorical force of delivery and increase persuasive qualities (Atkinson, 1989). The journalist in such an example juxtaposes 'punish(ing) terrorists', 'crack(ing) down on terror activists', 'crack(ing) down on' extremists and 'contain(ing) illegal religious activities'. These four actions are listed to impress international readers and show that the Chinese government will bring lawless and evil terrorists to justice for safety and stability in Chinese Muslim areas.

The topos of reality is used in the CD to reveal governmental paternalism. According to Wodak (2011), the scheme is typically the documentation of terrible social situations in discourse (e.g. poverty) and serves to justify the need for specific actions or decisions. In the CD corpus, the topos is used frequently to frame the topic category of terrorism/violence with the contribution value of 0.07 (out of 1.04) (Figure 2), and topic indicators include 'attacks', 'killed' and 'terrorists'. Regarding the use of the topos of reality, journalists emphasise actions in violence and consequences, and the scenarios position most Chinese Muslims as being in a worrying and dangerous situation. The plight of Chinese Muslims in the broadsheet justifies the government's actions to punish terrorists, who constitute a minority within the Chinese Muslim population. Simply put, the topos of reality reveals the vulnerability of a large population of Chinese Muslim citizens bred by the few evil and lawless Chinese Muslims and the needing of governmental interventions.

The topos thus construes governmental anti-terror campaigns that Beijing prioritises the necessity of its ingroup members and bring them security as well as constructs paternalism (cf. Le Grand and New, 2015;Saich, 2012;Wang and Liu, 2018). Example 4 about violence and terrorism elaborates on the use of the topos of reality to portray the victimised Chinese Muslim majority and the paternalistic Chinese government; topic indicators include 'riot', 'terrorist', 'extremist' and 'destroyed'. 'The riot has destroyed the spiritual support with which the terrorist, separatist and extremist forces cheated the people to participate in the so-called "Jihad"', Wang said. (7 July 2009) In the example, harm caused by terrorists to the majority of Chinese Muslim citizens is marked in the voice of 'Xinjiang Communist Party of China (CPC) chief'. They are first expressed in related to 'riot' and 'Jihad' and explicitly labelled as 'terrorist, separatist and extremist', and the labels explicate their Muslim identity and malicious nature. These terrorists, separatist and extremist Chinese Muslims are harmful to the Muslim majority in China because they 'destroyed the spiritual support' and 'cheated the people to participate in the so-called "Jihad"'. In other words, these Muslims are pictured as betraying Islam and misleading the majority (i.e. 'the people') to fundamentalism. The official's voice and the statement of harm caused by this minority to the Chinese Muslim majority build a boundary between the majority as an ingroup and the minority outgroup. CD justifies the necessity for the government to protect the ingroup by revealing its members' mental victimisation to the evil group. So, the topos of reality here predicts the government's activities of anti-terrorism previously discussed, through which the ideology is discernible.
sections.preview_head: The family of six had been living in a rented apartment that had barely enough space, Xirip said. She is happy to see local governments are now helping the family with two low-rent apartments. (5 February 2014) Chinese Muslims all explicitly convey sentiments consistent with the topos of happiness in these examples -'she is happy'. The clear expression of happiness distinguishes most Muslim citizens from the small number who are engaged in violence (cf. Example 4).

sections.paper_id: Ye and Thomas - 2020 - Paternalism in China Daily ’s coverage of Chinese Muslims (2001–2015)
paragraphs: The study addresses the need to more systematically study the media representation of Chinese Muslims. Specifically, it contributes to the systematic exploration of how governmental paternalism is discursively constructed as an ideology in the coverage of Chinese Muslims in CD and so representative of Beijing's voice.

Our analysis finds that the official English newspaper in China helps the Chinese government to disseminate governmental paternalism to international readers. Paternalistic discourse concentrates on the topic categories of social transformation in Chinese Muslim society, Islamic religion, violence in Xinjiang and China as well as the governmental response to violence. In this paternalistic discourse, the ideology is realised by a list of topoi that are constructed by other linguistic patterns. The topos of advantage is used consistently to reveal benefits to most Chinese Muslim citizens of the government's intervention, such as economic development and social security. Nominalisation is recurrent in the use of this topos in order to downgrade the engagement of the Chinese government in Muslims' daily life. The frequent use of topos of happiness suggests respect and obedience from Chinese Muslims driven by governmental assistance. Through the topos of responsibility and reality, the newspaper disseminates the information that Beijing maintains the duty of caring for its citizens and addresses their need to be protected from terrorism. The topos of responsibility is specifically realised by modal verbs and the strategy of lists of three. Governmental paternalism in the news about Chinese Muslims conforms with the paternalism of Beijing's policies towards ethnic minorities in which filial piety, the prioritisation of people's necessity, and the government's responsibility to take care of its citizens emerge (Cheng et al., 2017;Saich, 2012;Wang and Liu, 2018).

CD strongly suggests a political alignment between Chinese Muslims and Beijing through the list of topoi in the topic categories, which serves to legitimise governmental paternalism. The Chinese Muslim majority is portrayed as an ingroup to the government. They are treated as grateful recipients of the government's assistance to improve their living conditions, religious freedom and create a stable environment. The portrayal neither undermines their status as a community (Luqiu and Yang, 2018;Zheng, 2011) nor colours them as a victimised society (Witteborn, 2011). Beijing is portrayed as paternal to the majority of its Chinese Muslim citizens through assisting their daily lives and protecting them from the peril of terrorists, and its guardianship implies inclusiveness of Chinese Muslims and ethnic unity. CD also attributes violence in China to terrorists; a small number of Chinese Muslims who incite riots and explosions in Xinjiang, Kunming and some other regions in China. They are portrayed as a group that victimises the Muslim majority and outgroups to the government, whereby justifying Beijing's acts of anti-terrorism as the prioritisation of people's needs for a stable and safe society. The appreciative people's security by the government reveals the portrayal of the Chinese Muslim majority as its ingroup members and legitimises Chinese paternalistic policies in the region.

This study contributes to understanding precisely how the major Chinese Englishlanguage state newspaper discursively represents Beijing's governmental paternalism towards Chinese Muslims. In contrast to CD's positive construction, political scientists (Anand, 2018;Holdstock, 2015;Kung, 2006;Mackerras, 2014;Shan and Weng, 2010) have explored wider concerns about the autonomy of Chinese Muslims in China under such policies. Further study could explore the distance between the portrayal of paternalism in CD and the ongoing realities of the region. For instance, future linguistic exploration could focus on the reliability and representability of paternalistic discourse, through interviews or surveys with Chinese Muslims. The scope of future research could also be broadened to incorporate multiple Chinese newspapers both in Chinese and English for more comprehensive appreciation as to how paternalism is backgrounded as an ideology by topics and topoi in the coverage of Chinese Muslims.
